subcategory: South American tropical climbing leafy vegetable

planting.note: Plant aerial tubers or root divisions in spring in full sun in fertile, well-drained, moderately moist soil in zone 9–12, or as an annual in zone 6–8 after last frost; Madeira Vine (Mignonette Vine) is a fast-growing tropical perennial climber from South America — cultivated for its edible, fleshy, heart-shaped leaves (rich in vitamins and minerals, cooked like spinach) and small, warty aerial tubers — with fragrant white flower spikes and remarkable speed of growth in warm conditions.

directions: Plant Anredera cordifolia tubers or divisions in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; provide a trellis, fence, or other support as it is a vigorous climber; water regularly during active growth; harvest young, tender leaves as needed throughout the warm season; contain carefully as the aerial tubers can spread and the plant is invasive in warm climates. Madeira Vine is native to tropical South America and has spread as a culinary crop throughout Asia, where the leaves are eaten as a nutritious cooked green vegetable in the same manner as spinach or Malabar spinach — particularly popular in the Philippines (alugbati companion), Taiwan, and Indonesia. The succulent, heart-shaped leaves are harvested continuously from the vigorous vines and cooked briefly in stir-fries, soups, and wilted salads; they have a mild, slightly mucilaginous texture similar to New Zealand spinach. The plant produces numerous small, warty aerial tubers along the stems as well as underground tubers, all of which are edible cooked. WARNING: Madeira Vine is classified as a serious environmental weed in Australia, New Zealand, South Africa, and some US states; never plant near natural areas, and dispose of all plant material including tiny aerial tubers carefully, as even small fragments can establish new plants. Grow in containers or in contained raised beds in frost-free areas.
sources: Taiwan Agricultural Research Institute — Anredera cordifolia cultivation
University of Queensland — Anredera cordifolia invasive weed alert

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in full sun in fertile, well-drained, deeply dug soil in zone 7–12; Silver-seed Gourd (Cushaw Squash, Striped Cushaw, Mixta Gourd) is an ancient Mexican heritage cucurbit — the fourth domesticated Cucurbita species — cultivated for at least 7,000 years in Mesoamerica and the traditional base for Mexican pepita (pumpkin seed) oil, tamale fillings, and pumpkin candies; bearing large, distinctively curved-neck or round-and-striped fruits with flat, silvery-white seeds.

directions: Direct sow Cucurbita argyrosperma seeds in hills 4–6 inches apart after soil warms to 70°F in full sun in deep, fertile, well-drained soil; provide ample space as vines spread 10–15 feet; water deeply once or twice weekly and side-dress with compost at vine set; harvest when rinds are hard and stems begin to cork. Silver-seed Gourd is the least-known of the four domesticated Cucurbita species but arguably the most heat-tolerant — better adapted to hot, dry summers of the US Southwest, Mexico, and Central America than C. pepo or C. maxima. It encompasses the familiar Cushaw squash (large, curved-neck, striped with light and dark green) and many traditional Mexican varieties grown specifically for their large, flat, silverish seeds (pepitas) used in the classic Mexican mole negro, pepita oil production, and traditional candies (dulce de pepita). The flesh of the large fruits is mild and sweet, excellent for pies and tamale fillings; the seeds are dried, shelled, and used as a significant protein and oil source in Mexican cuisine. Cushaw squash resists vine borers and powdery mildew better than many other Cucurbita species and performs excellently in zone 8–12 heat. Several named heirloom varieties are available including 'Green Striped Cushaw', 'White Cushaw', and 'Jonathan' (round-fruited).
sources: Nabhan, Gary Paul, 'Enduring Seeds' (North Point Press, 1989)
Seed Savers Exchange — Cucurbita argyrosperma heritage varieties

subcategory: West African tropical leafy oilseed vegetable vine

planting.note: Direct sow seeds after last frost in full sun in fertile, moist, well-drained, deep loamy soil in zone 9–12; Fluted Gourd (Ugu, Fluted Pumpkin) is one of the most important and widely grown leafy vegetables of southern Nigeria and West Africa — a fast-growing tropical vine bearing very large, ribbed gourds and large, soft leaves that are harvested continuously as the primary leafy vegetable in Egusi soup, Ofe Owerri, and other classic Nigerian soups; the large seeds are pressed for fluted pumpkin seed oil or eaten cooked.

directions: Sow Telfairia occidentalis seeds directly in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun; provide a strong trellis or fence for the vigorous vine; harvest young, tender leaves and shoot tips continuously from 4–6 weeks after planting for use as a cooked green vegetable; harvest mature gourds for seed when the skin turns yellow-green. Fluted Gourd (known as 'ugu' in Igbo) is arguably the single most important leafy vegetable in southern Nigeria — a vine plant that is grown in virtually every family plot and kitchen garden throughout Igboland and beyond. The large, prominently ribbed leaves (resembling pumpkin leaves but more tender and palatable) are harvested in volume and sliced into fine strips before being added to soups and stews at the end of cooking to preserve their vivid green color. The leaves are especially important in Egusi soup (with ground melon seeds), Ofe Onugbu, and Ofe Owerri. The enormous, ribbed, green-striped gourds can weigh 10–30 kg and contain large, flat, oval seeds rich in oil and protein; the seeds are dried, shelled, and eaten as a protein source or pressed for cooking oil (fluted pumpkin seed oil). Unlike most gourds, Telfairia occidentalis has separate male and female plants; female plants bear the gourds while male plants are grown primarily for continuous leaf harvest. In zone 9–12 it grows as a perennial vine.
sources: Federal Ministry of Agriculture Nigeria — ugu cultivation guide
University of Ibadan Faculty of Agriculture — Telfairia occidentalis

planting.note: Plant a whole, lightly germinated fruit of White Chayote on its side, pointed end down at a 45-degree angle with half the fruit above soil, in full sun in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in zone 9–12; White Chayote is a traditional Mexican and Central American white-skinned variety of chayote (Sechium edule var. virens alba) — smoother-skinned and more delicate in flavor than the common green-spined chayote — popular in traditional Mexican cuisine and preferred in some markets for its attractive pale-cream to white skin and mildly sweet flavor.

directions: Plant a whole, slightly sprouted White Chayote fruit on its side with the sprouting end just above the soil level in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in full sun; provide a very sturdy trellis or fence for the vigorous climbing vine; water deeply once or twice weekly during active growth; harvest fruits when they are 3–5 inches long for the most tender, mild-flavored results. White Chayote is a traditional variety of the widely grown Mesoamerican perennial vine vegetable, distinguished by its smooth to very lightly furrowed, cream-white to pale-green skin compared to the spiny, dark-green skin of the more common commercial variety. Like all chayote, it is planted by burying the entire fruit — the seed is inside and too large to separate without harm — and the vine emerges from the fruit and climbs vigorously. In zone 10–12, the vine grows as a long-lived perennial with a massive tuberous root system; a well-established vine can produce hundreds of fruits per season. White Chayote fruits are notably smoother and many cooks consider the flavor slightly milder and sweeter than spiny green varieties; they are used in all the same ways — raw in salads, steamed, boiled, stuffed, or pickled. The young leaves and shoot tips, tuberous roots, and seeds are all edible as with all chayote. An excellent year-round producer in Gulf Coast zone 9 gardens, dying back to the ground in mild frost and re-emerging in spring.
sources: CIMMYT Mesoamerican vegetable crops database — Sechium edule varieties
Mexico's SAGARPA — Sechium edule cultivation guide
Rare Fruit Council International — chayote varieties

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in full sun in fertile, well-drained, deep loamy soil in zone 9–12; White-seed Melon (Aro Egusi, Round Egusi, Mann's Cucumeropsis) is a West African cucurbit grown primarily for its large, flat, white-seeded fruits — the seeds dried, ground, or pressed for oil to make the foundational West African Egusi Soup, which is served throughout Nigeria, Ghana, Cameroon, and Côte d'Ivoire — distinct from the better-known egusi watermelon (Citrullus lanatus var. citroides).

directions: Direct sow Cucumeropsis mannii seeds in hills in full sun in rich, deep, well-drained soil after temperatures are consistently warm; train vines on a trellis or allow to sprawl on the ground; harvest mature fruits when the skin yellows and stems cork; dry seeds thoroughly before storage or use. White-seed Melon is one of two major 'egusi' cucurbits of West Africa (the other being the egusi watermelon, Citrullus lanatus var. citroides), distinguished by its round, smooth, cream-green fruits and large, flat, distinctly white seeds. The plant is a trailing or climbing annual vine with lobed, cucumber-like leaves and small yellow flowers — grown entirely for its seeds, which are the primary ingredient in Egusi Soup, the beloved national dish of Nigeria and a staple across much of West and Central Africa. The seeds are dried, then ground into a paste or coarse meal and fried in palm oil with onions and tomatoes before adding stock and leafy greens (typically bitter leaf or pumpkin leaf) to create the rich, deeply savory egusi soup. Seeds can also be pressed to produce egusi oil, a golden cooking oil with a mild, nutty flavor. The flesh of the fruits is not eaten (bitter and unpalatable) — the crop is exclusively a seed crop. An important protein and fat source in West African diets where meat is expensive.
sources: FAO — Cucumeropsis mannii in West African food systems
IITA (International Institute of Tropical Agriculture) — egusi cultivation in West Africa

subcategory: East African tropical oilseed and edible seed vine

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in spring after frosts in full sun in fertile, moist, well-drained, deep loamy soil in zone 10–12; Oyster Nut is an enormous tropical vine from East Africa — native to Tanzania, Uganda, Kenya, and Mozambique — grown for its exceptionally large seeds (resembling giant sesame seeds) that are eaten roasted, boiled, or ground into a protein-rich paste; a close relative of the West African Fluted Gourd (Telfairia occidentalis) but larger-fruited, with fruits weighing up to 70 kg.

directions: Plant Telfairia pedata seeds in fertile, deep, well-drained, moist soil in full sun; provide the sturdiest support structure possible as this vine can climb 30–50 feet and bears massive, heavy fruits; water deeply twice weekly during active growth; harvest mature fruits when the skin yellows and splits; dry and shell seeds before eating. Oyster Nut is among the most impressively large of all tropical vines — a forest-margin climber capable of ascending trees to 100 feet and producing enormous, football-shaped to cylindrical gourds that can weigh 40–70 kilograms (100–150 lbs), making them among the largest fruits of any plant. The fruits contain 30–100 large, flat, ovoid seeds (resembling giant sesame or pumpkin seeds) with a thin, papery shell and rich, oily, protein-dense kernel. The seeds are roasted and eaten as a snack, ground into a protein-rich paste for cooking (similar to egusi), or pressed for a cooking oil with a mild, distinctive flavor. In East Africa, particularly in Tanzania and Kenya, Oyster Nut is an important food security crop and commercial oil crop; efforts are underway to expand cultivation. The vines require massive, strong trellises or tree support and will break lightweight structures; plant near a sturdy tree or build a purpose-made strong support before planting.
sources: CABI Invasive Species Compendium — Telfairia pedata (non-invasive East African native)
